title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: members, contributors, leaders pledge make participation community harassment-free experience everyone, regardless age, body size, visible invisible disability, ethnicity, sex characteristics, gender identity expression, level experience, education, socio-economic status, nationality, personal appearance, race, caste, color, religion, sexual identity orientation. pledge act interact ways contribute open, welcoming, diverse, inclusive, healthy community.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Examples behavior contributes positive environment community include: Demonstrating empathy kindness toward people respectful differing opinions, viewpoints, experiences Giving gracefully accepting constructive feedback Accepting responsibility apologizing affected mistakes, learning experience Focusing best just us individuals, overall community Examples unacceptable behavior include: use sexualized language imagery, sexual attention advances kind Trolling, insulting derogatory comments, personal political attacks Public private harassment Publishing others’ private information, physical email address, without explicit permission conduct reasonably considered inappropriate professional setting

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community leaders responsible clarifying enforcing standards acceptable behavior take appropriate fair corrective action response behavior deem inappropriate, threatening, offensive, harmful. Community leaders right responsibility remove, edit, reject comments, commits, code, wiki edits, issues, contributions aligned Code Conduct, communicate reasons moderation decisions appropriate.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Code Conduct applies within community spaces, also applies individual officially representing community public spaces. Examples representing community include using official e-mail address, posting via official social media account, acting appointed representative online offline event.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Instances abusive, harassing, otherwise unacceptable behavior may reported community leaders responsible enforcement https://github.com/SignorinoY/survtrans/issues. complaints reviewed investigated promptly fairly. community leaders obligated respect privacy security reporter incident.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community leaders follow Community Impact Guidelines determining consequences action deem violation Code Conduct:

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community Impact: Use inappropriate language behavior deemed unprofessional unwelcome community. Consequence: private, written warning community leaders, providing clarity around nature violation explanation behavior inappropriate. public apology may requested.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community Impact: violation single incident series actions. Consequence: warning consequences continued behavior. interaction people involved, including unsolicited interaction enforcing Code Conduct, specified period time. includes avoiding interactions community spaces well external channels like social media. Violating terms may lead temporary permanent ban.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community Impact: serious violation community standards, including sustained inappropriate behavior. Consequence: temporary ban sort interaction public communication community specified period time. public private interaction people involved, including unsolicited interaction enforcing Code Conduct, allowed period. Violating terms may lead permanent ban.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community Impact: Demonstrating pattern violation community standards, including sustained inappropriate behavior, harassment individual, aggression toward disparagement classes individuals. Consequence: permanent ban sort public interaction within community.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Code Conduct adapted Contributor Covenant, version 2.1, available https://www.contributor-covenant.org/version/2/1/code_of_conduct.html. Community Impact Guidelines inspired [Mozilla’s code conduct enforcement ladder][https://github.com/mozilla/inclusion]. answers common questions code conduct, see FAQ https://www.contributor-covenant.org/faq. Translations available https://www.contributor-covenant.org/translations.

what: Example 1: Homogeneous Baseline Hazard Function and Heterogeneous Effects
title: The Generation of Simulated Survival Data
code: n_features <- 10 n_samples_source <- 900 n_samples_target <- 100  mu <- rep(0, n_features) sigma <- diag(n_features) beta <- rep(1, n_features) names(beta) <- paste0("X", 1:n_features) eta <- c(0.5, rep(0, n_features - 1))  cov_source <- data.frame(   id = 1:n_samples_source, mvrnorm(n_samples_source, mu = mu, Sigma = sigma) ) time_source <- simsurv(   lambdas = 1 / 3, gammas = 3, x = cov_source, betas = beta, maxt = 3 ) cov_source$group <- 1 time_source$group <- 1  cov_target <- data.frame(   id = 1:n_samples_target, mvrnorm(n_samples_target, mu = mu, Sigma = sigma) ) time_target <- simsurv(   lambdas = 1 / 3, gammas = 3, x = cov_target, betas = beta + eta, maxt = 3 ) cov_target$group <- 2 time_target$group <- 2  covs <- rbind(cov_source, cov_target) times <- rbind(time_source, time_target) sim1 <- left_join(covs, times, by = c("id", "group")) %>%   rename(time = "eventtime") %>%   mutate(id = seq_len(nrow(.))) %>%   select(id, group, time, status, X1:X10)

what: Example 2: Heterogeneous Baseline Hazard Function and Heterogeneous Effects
title: The Generation of Simulated Survival Data
code: n_groups <- 10 n_samples <- 100 n_features <- 10  mu <- rep(0, n_features) sigma <- diag(n_features) beta <- rep(c(1, 0), n_features / 2) names(beta) <- paste0("X", 1:n_features) etas <- rbind(   rep(c(0, 1), n_features / 2),   rep(c(0, -1), n_features / 2) ) lambdas <- c(1 / 3, 1 / 2) gammas <- c(3, 2)  covs <- c() times <- c() for (k in 1:n_groups) {   cov <- data.frame(     id = 1:n_samples, mvrnorm(n_samples, mu = mu, Sigma = sigma)   )   time <- simsurv(     lambdas = lambdas[k %% 2 + 1], gammas = gammas[k %% 2 + 1],     x = cov, betas = beta + etas[k %% 2 + 1, ], maxt = 3   )   cov$group <- k   time$group <- k   covs <- rbind(covs, cov)   times <- rbind(times, time) }  sim2 <- left_join(covs, times, by = c("id", "group")) %>%   rename(time = "eventtime") %>%   mutate(id = seq_len(nrow(.))) %>%   select(id, group, time, status, X1:X10)

title: The Generation of Simulated Survival Data
code: n_features <- 10 n_samples <- 1000  mu <- rep(0, n_features) sigma <- diag(n_features) beta <- c(1, 0.5, rep(0, n_features - 2)) names(beta) <- paste0("X", 1:n_features)  covs <- data.frame(   id = 1:n_samples, mvrnorm(n_samples, mu = mu, Sigma = sigma) ) times <- simsurv(   lambdas = 1 / 3, gammas = 3, x = covs, betas = beta, maxt = 3 )  sim_sparse <- left_join(covs, times, by = "id") %>%   rename(time = "eventtime") %>%   mutate(id = seq_len(nrow(.))) %>%   select(id, time, status, X1:X10)

code: coxtrans(   formula,   data,   group,   lambda1 = 0,   lambda2 = 0,   penalty = c("lasso", "MCP", "SCAD"),   gamma = switch(penalty, SCAD = 3.7, MCP = 3, 1),   rho = 1.1,   init,   control,   ... )

title: Transfer Learning of Multi-source for Cox proportional hazards model — coxtrans
text: formula formula expression regression models, form response ~ predictors. response must survival object returned Surv function. data data frame containing variables model. group factor specifying group sample. lambda1 non-negative value specifying sparisity penalty parameter. default 0. lambda2 non-negative value specifying group penalty parameter. default 0. penalty character string specifying penalty function. default "lasso". options "MCP" "SCAD". gamma non-negative value specifying penalty parameter. default 3.7 SCAD 3.0 MCP. rho value (2, 10) specifying expansion factor augmented Lagrangian's penalty parameter. default 1.1. init numeric vector specifying initial value coefficients. default zero vector. control Object class survtrans_control containing control parameters fitting algorithm. Default survtrans_control(...). ... arguments passed survtrans_control.

what:
Simulated Group Survival Data: Homogeneous Baseline Hazard Function and
Heterogeneous Effects — sim1
title:
Simulated Group Survival Data: Homogeneous Baseline Hazard Function and
Heterogeneous Effects — sim1
text: dataset containing 2 groups survival data, baseline hazards homogeneous effects heterogeneous. first group, also called source group, contains 900 individuals, second group, also called target group, contains 100 individuals. survival time group \(\) generated according following models: $$\lambda^{()}(t)=\lambda_{0}(t)\exp\left(x^\top(\beta+\eta^{()}) \right),$$ \(\lambda_{0}(t)\) represents baseline hazard, \(\beta+\eta^{()}\) represents effects group \(\), \(x\) represents covariates. covariates \(x=(x_{1},\ldots,x_{10})\) generated multivariate normal distribution mean 0 covariance matrix \(\Sigma=I_{10}\). baseline hazard \(\lambda_{0}(t)=t^2\), effects $$\eta^{()}=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}(0,\ldots,0),&=1,\\ (0.5,0,\ldots,0),&=2.\end{array}\right.$$ Additionally, \(\beta=(1,\ldots,1)\). maximum censoring time fixed 3, censoring rate approximately 20%.

title:
Simulated Group Survival Data: Homogeneous Baseline Hazard Function and
Heterogeneous Effects — sim1
text: data frame 1000 rows 14 variables: id Individual identifier, 1-1000. group Group indicator, 1=Source, 2=Target. time Survival time. status Status indicator, 0=censored, 1=event. X1 covariate 1. X2 covariate 2. X3 covariate 3. X4 covariate 4. X5 covariate 5. X6 covariate 6. X7 covariate 7. X8 covariate 8. X9 covariate 9. X10 covariate 10.

title:
Simulated Group Survival Data: Homogeneous Baseline Hazard Function and
Heterogeneous Effects — sim1
text: ../articles/simulate-data.html

what:
Simulated Group Survival Data: Heterogeneous Baseline Hazard Function and
Heterogeneous Effects — sim2
title:
Simulated Group Survival Data: Heterogeneous Baseline Hazard Function and
Heterogeneous Effects — sim2
text: dataset containing 10 groups survival data, part effects baseline hazards heterogeneous. group contains 100 individuals. survival time \(T\) group \(\) generated according following models: $$\lambda^{()}(t)=\lambda_{0}^{()}(t)\exp\left(x^\top(\beta+\eta^{()}) \right),$$ \(\lambda_{0}^{()}(t)\) represents baseline hazard group \(\), \(\beta+\eta^{()}\) represents effects group \(\), \(x\) represents covariates. covariates \(x=(x_{1},\ldots,x_{10})\) generated multivariate normal distribution mean 0 covariance matrix \(\Sigma=I_{10}\). baseline hazards generated follows: $$\lambda_{0}^{()}(t)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}t^2,&=1,3,\dots,9,\\ t,&=2,4,\dots,10.\end{array}\right.$$ effects \(\eta^{()}\) generated follows: $$\eta^{()}=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}(0,1,\ldots,0,1),&=1,\dots,9,\\ (0,-1,\ldots,0,-1),&=2,\dots,10.\end{array}\right.$$ Additionally, \(\beta=(1,0,\ldots,1,0)\). maximum censoring time fixed 3, censoring rate approximately 20%.

title:
Simulated Group Survival Data: Heterogeneous Baseline Hazard Function and
Heterogeneous Effects — sim2
text: data frame 1000 rows 14 variables: id Individual identifier, 1-1000. group Group indicator, 1-10. time Survival time. status Status indicator, 0=censored, 1=event. X1 covariate 1. X2 covariate 2. X3 covariate 3. X4 covariate 4. X5 covariate 5. X6 covariate 6. X7 covariate 7. X8 covariate 8. X9 covariate 9. X10 covariate 10.

title:
Simulated Group Survival Data: Heterogeneous Baseline Hazard Function and
Heterogeneous Effects — sim2

what: Simulated Survival Data: Sparse Effects — sim_sparse
title: Simulated Survival Data: Sparse Effects — sim_sparse
text: dataset survival data sparse effects. survival time generated according following model: $$\lambda(t)=\lambda_{0}(t)\exp\left(x^\top\beta\right),$$ \(\lambda_{0}(t)\) represents baseline hazard, \(\beta\) represents effects, \(x\) represents covariates. covariates \(x=(x_{1},\ldots,x_{10})\) generated multivariate normal distribution mean 0 covariance matrix \(\Sigma=I_{10}\). baseline hazard \(\lambda_{0}(t)=t^2\), effects \(\beta=(1,0.5,0,\ldots,0)\). maximum censoring time fixed 3, censoring rate approximately 20%.

X4 covariate 4. X5 covariate 5. X6 covariate 6. X7 covariate 7. X8 covariate 8. X9 covariate 9. X10 covariate 10.
